sections.title: Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read three extracts which are all concerned in some way with well-being or lifestyle. For questions 1–6, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.
sections.passageHtml:
Extract 1: The perfect campsite

It is a standard gag in comedies: the family turns up at a campsite only to find it is a mudbath populated by football-playing teenagers, or a dustbowl where the only shade is provided by the exhaust of a diesel generator. But we were lucky. Our site, selected at random from a directory, was a small farm with a couple of fields set aside for campers, overlooking a quiet stretch of river. Best of all, we had the place to ourselves. We pitched our tent in the shadow of a pear tree, and by late afternoon were sitting in our folding chairs, listening to the gentle gurgle of the water and watching the swallows. For the first time in months, I felt my shoulders dropping from their level with my ears. I began to think we might survive the week after all.

Then the rain started. The weather forecast had been promising, but within minutes the sky was the colour of a bruised plum. In no time the field was sodden, and we retreated to the dry but cramped security of the tent. There was nothing to do but lie on our sleeping bags and wait. Yet, as the rain drummed on the canvas, I found myself thinking that it didn’t really matter. We had a good tent, plenty of books, and the city was far away. The weather would do what it wanted, and we would do what we wanted. It was a good feeling.

Extract 2: Writing for the screen

My first screenwriting job was a feature film about a bank robbery. I was hired because I had written a couple of novels that had done okay, and the producer thought I could bring some 'literary depth' to the project. It was a flattering idea, but I soon realized that writing a screenplay has about as much in common with writing a novel as playing the violin has with playing the drums. In a novel, you can spend pages describing a character’s internal state, or the history of a town, or the quality of the light at dusk. In a screenplay, if you can’t see it or hear it, it doesn’t exist. You have to tell the story entirely through action and dialogue.

It was a hard lesson to learn, and I made every mistake in the book. My first draft was twice as long as it should have been, and was packed with scenes where characters sat around talking about their feelings. The director, a veteran of dozens of action films, was polite but firm. 'This is great stuff,' he said, pointing to a long monologue I had written for the protagonist, 'but on screen, this character is a dinosaur. If he talks this much, the audience will fall asleep. We need to show what he’s feeling, not have him talk about it.' He was right, of course. We cut the monologue and replaced it with a single, silent shot of the character looking at a photograph. It was far more effective. The experience taught me that screenwriting is a collaborative art, and that sometimes the best writing is the writing you don’t do.

Extract 3: Modern relationships

It is often said that we live in an individualistic age, where traditional social bonds have weakened and people are increasingly isolated. According to this view, the rise of the internet and social media has only accelerated this trend, replacing real-world relationships with superficial online connections. But is this really the case? A recent study of friendship patterns suggests a more complex picture. While it is true that people move house more often and change jobs more frequently than their grandparents did, the basic human need for connection remains unchanged. We still seek out friends, not because we have to, but because we want to. Friendship is a voluntary relationship, based on mutual affection and shared interests, and as such it plays a crucial role in our psychological well-being.

Indeed, the study suggests that modern friendships may in some ways be more resilient than those of the past. In agricultural societies, friendships were often determined by proximity: you were friends with your neighbours because they were the only people around. If you fell out with them, or if they moved away, you were on your own. Today, we are free to choose our friends from a much wider pool, and thanks to modern technology, we can maintain those friendships even if we live on opposite sides of the world. Far from isolating us, the modern world may actually give us the tools to build stronger, more meaningful connections than our ancestors ever could.

questions.instruction: 1. In the first paragraph, the writer's main purpose is to
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1, Trang 8 Chọn A — 'describe the campsite he had chosen.'. Trong đoạn đầu tiên, người viết mô tả chi tiết địa điểm cắm trại của mình: 'Our site, selected at random from a directory, was a small farm with a couple of fields set aside for campers, overlooking a quiet stretch of river...'. Người viết đối lập khu cắm trại thực tế yên bình này với những cảnh tượng tồi tệ thường thấy trong phim hài. ❌ B sai vì người viết không so sánh các địa điểm mà chỉ nói về địa điểm của mình. ❌ C sai vì việc dựng lều chỉ là một chi tiết nhỏ chứ không phải mục đích chính của đoạn văn. ❌ D sai vì người viết chỉ đề cập đến việc cảm thấy thư giãn ở cuối đoạn chứ không phải mục đích chính là giải thích tại sao cần kỳ nghỉ. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: A. describe the campsite he had chosen.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. describe the campsite he had chosen.
B. compare different campsites he had visited.
C. explain how he pitched his tent.
D. show why he needed a holiday.

questions.instruction: 2. The writer was not worried about the rain because
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1, Trang 8 Chọn C — 'he felt sure the weather would soon improve.'. Tác giả viết: 'The weather forecast had been promising... yet, as the rain drummed on the canvas, I found myself thinking that it didn’t really matter... The weather would do what it wanted, and we would do what we wanted.' Điều này thể hiện thái độ lạc quan, không bận tâm vì tin rằng thời tiết sẽ sớm ổn định lại hoặc đơn giản là họ có sự chuẩn bị tốt để tận hưởng dù mưa. ❌ A sai vì lều của họ khô ráo ('dry security of the tent') nhưng đó không phải lý do duy nhất khiến tác giả không lo lắng. ❌ B sai vì tác giả mang theo nhiều sách nhưng không nói việc đọc sách làm biến mất nỗi lo. ❌ D sai vì không có thông tin nào nói tác giả thích thời tiết mưa khi đi cắm trại. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: C. he felt sure the weather would soon improve.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. his tent was completely waterproof.
B. he had brought plenty of books to read.
C. he felt sure the weather would soon improve.
D. he enjoyed the sound of rain on the tent.
questions.source: Cambridge Certificate in Advanced English 2, Cambridge University Press, Part 1: Multiple Choice (Reading)

questions.instruction: 3. The writer mentions the 'dinosaur' in line 16 in order to emphasise
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1, Trang 9 Chọn D — 'the outdated nature of his work.'. Đạo diễn nói về nhân vật trong kịch bản đầu tiên của tác giả: 'on screen, this character is a dinosaur. If he talks this much, the audience will fall asleep.' Từ 'dinosaur' (khủng long) ở đây được dùng ẩn dụ để chỉ sự lỗi thời, lạc hậu trong cách xây dựng nhân vật quá nhiều lời thoại dài dòng cho một bộ phim điện ảnh hiện đại. ❌ A sai vì 'dinosaur' không nhằm nói về kích thước của kịch bản. ❌ B sai vì không nói về tính cách thô bạo của nhân vật. ❌ C sai vì không ám chỉ sự thiếu thực tế của cốt truyện. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

questions.instruction: 4. According to the writer, one advantage of screenwriting is
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1, Trang 9 Chọn B — 'the collaboration with others.'. Cuối đoạn 2, tác giả đúc kết: 'The experience taught me that screenwriting is a collaborative art, and that sometimes the best writing is the writing you don’t do.' Khác với viết tiểu thuyết đơn độc, viết kịch bản phim mang lại lợi thế từ việc hợp tác cùng đạo diễn và đoàn làm phim để tạo ra tác phẩm tinh gọn, hiệu quả hơn. ❌ A sai vì viết kịch bản đòi hỏi khắt khe hơn về hình ảnh và âm thanh chứ không hề dễ dàng hơn. ❌ C sai vì không đề cập đến tự do tài chính. ❌ D sai vì viết kịch bản phải tuân theo sự điều chỉnh của đạo diễn/nhà sản xuất nên không thể nói là có nhiều quyền kiểm soát hơn. Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: B. the collaboration with others.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. the ease of the writing process.
D. the control the writer has over the final product.

questions.instruction: 5. In the writer's opinion, the main reason why people become friends is
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1, Trang 10 Chọn A — 'a mutual need for company.'. Ở đoạn 1 của trích đoạn 3 có viết: 'the basic human need for connection remains unchanged. We still seek out friends, not because we have to, but because we want to. Friendship is a voluntary relationship, based on mutual affection and shared interests...'. Như vậy, nhu cầu kết nối và bầu bạn lẫn nhau là lý do cốt lõi nhất. ❌ B sai vì tình bạn là tự nguyện chứ không phải do áp lực xã hội. ❌ C sai vì các mối quan tâm thực tế (như công việc, địa lý) không phải lý do chính yếu được tác giả nhấn mạnh. ❌ D sai vì chia sẻ bí mật chỉ là một phần nhỏ chứ không phải lý do chính. questions.answerLocation: A. a mutual need for company.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.
options: A. a mutual need for company.
D. the desire to share secrets.

questions.instruction: 6. In the second paragraph, the writer suggests that our ancestors
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1, Trang 10 Chọn C — 'had friendships that were more stable.'. Tác giả phân tích rằng trong xã hội nông nghiệp trước đây, tình bạn bị giới hạn bởi khoảng cách địa lý ('determined by proximity'): bạn làm bạn với hàng xóm vì họ là những người duy nhất xung quanh. Mối quan hệ này có tính chất cố định và lâu dài hơn do ít có sự di chuyển hay thay đổi lựa chọn. ❌ A sai vì ngày nay chúng ta có nhiều sự lựa chọn bạn bè hơn chứ không phải tổ tiên có nhiều lựa chọn hơn. ❌ B sai vì tổ tiên không thể duy trì tình bạn từ xa do thiếu công nghệ. ❌ D sai vì không có cơ sở nào nói họ coi trọng tình bạn hơn con người hiện đại. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.
questions.answerLocation: C. had friendships that were more stable.. Evidence cue: use this answer-key option with the passage/audio paraphrase; confirm the full claim, not just a repeated keyword.

sections.title: Part 2: Gapped Text (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read an extract from a story. Six paragraphs have been removed from the extract. Choose from the paragraphs A–G the one which fits each gap (7–12). There is one extra paragraph which you do not need to use.
sections.passageHtml:
The Magic Lute

The morning was crisp and clear, the kind of autumn day that makes you feel glad to be alive. I walked down the narrow path that led from my cottage to the river, carrying my lute in its worn leather case. The instrument had belonged to my grandfather, and though it was old and slightly scarred, it still possessed a sweet, resonant tone that no modern instrument could match.

Gap 7

I reached my favourite spot, a grassy bank shaded by an ancient willow tree whose branches swept the surface of the water. I sat down, opened the case, and lifted the lute out. As I tuned the strings, the familiar scent of old wood and varnish drifted up, bringing back memories of long winter evenings spent sitting by my grandfather’s fireplace, watching his fingers fly across the fretboard.

Gap 8

I began to play a slow, melancholic melody, a traditional air that my grandfather had taught me when I was just a boy. The music seemed to float out over the water, blending with the rustle of the wind in the reeds and the gentle murmur of the current. For a few minutes, I was completely lost in the sound, oblivious to everything else.

Gap 9

Startled, I stopped playing and looked up. A young woman was standing a few yards away, watching me. She was dressed in a long, dark green cloak, and her hair was the colour of copper. She had an unusual, striking face, with eyes that seemed to shift colour in the light, changing from green to amber.

Gap 10

'I didn't mean to interrupt you,' she said, her voice soft and clear. 'But I have never heard that tune played like that before. It was beautiful.' I thanked her, feeling a warm flush of pride. 'My grandfather taught it to me,' I explained. 'It’s a very old tune from the hills.'

Gap 11

She walked closer, her movements silent and graceful, and sat down on a nearby rock. 'May I see it?' she asked, pointing to the lute. I hesitated for a moment — I rarely let anyone touch the instrument — but there was something in her manner that disarmed me. I handed it over.

Gap 12

She took the lute in her lap, her fingers brushing the wood with a reverence that matched my own. She did not play a melody, but simply plucked a single string. The note hung in the air, incredibly pure and long-lasting, far louder and deeper than any note I had ever managed to coax from the instrument. It was as if the lute had suddenly found its true voice.

Paragraphs Options:

A. My hands adjusted the pegs automatically, my fingers remembering the precise tension required for each note. It was a ritual I had performed thousands of times, yet it never failed to bring a sense of calm and order to my mind.
B. But the peace of the morning was suddenly broken by the sound of a dry branch snapping behind me. The noise was sharp and distinct, echoing in the quiet air of the valley.
C. I watched as she turned the instrument over in her hands, examining the delicate inlay around the soundhole and the worn carvings on the pegbox. It was clear she knew something about the craft of instrument making.
D. She stood there in silence for a moment, as if waiting for me to say something, but I was too surprised by her sudden appearance to speak. The green cloak she wore seemed to blend perfectly with the foliage of the willow.
E. She nodded slowly, her gaze fixed on the instrument. 'He must have been a fine player,' she said. 'And he must have cared for this instrument very much. You can feel the history in the wood.'
F. The river itself was unusually high that morning, swollen by the recent rains in the hills. The water was muddy and turbulent, carrying branches and debris down towards the plains.
G. I had spent the previous evening polishing the dark wood, removing the dust that had accumulated during months of neglect. Now, in the bright sunlight, the maple back and spruce top gleamed with a deep, warm luster.

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct paragraph from A–G to fill each gap.

questions.instruction: 7. Gap 7
questions.explanation: **Nguồn:** Cambridge CAE 2 — Test 1, Trang 11 Chọn G. Đoạn trước Gap 7 mô tả về chiếc đàn lute trong bao da cũ ('carrying my lute in its worn leather case') đã cũ và trầy xước. Đoạn G nối tiếp bằng việc giải thích người viết đã dành cả tối hôm trước để đánh bóng gỗ đàn ('polishing the dark wood') giúp nó sáng bóng dưới ánh nắng mặt trời ('gleamed with a deep, warm luster'). Đoạn sau Gap 7 tiếp tục tả cảnh khi đến bờ sông và mở hộp đàn ra ('opened the case, and lifted the lute out'). Method: read both sides of the gap and choose the option that fits meaning, collocation and grammar. Trap: reject distractors that are close in meaning but do not fit the pattern or register in context.

sections.title: Part 3: Multiple Choice (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read a magazine article about the crime writer P.D. James. For questions 13–19, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text.
sections.passageHtml:
P.D. James: The Queen of Crime

P.D. James, the author of more than a dozen bestselling detective novels, is often described as the successor to Agatha Christie. It is a comparison she accepts with a mix of gratitude and mild irritation. 'Agatha Christie was a superb storyteller, and she did more than anyone to popularize the genre,' James says, sitting in the drawing room of her London home. 'But her novels are essentially puzzles. They are two-dimensional. I want to write novels that are three-dimensional, where the characters have pasts and futures, and where the setting is as important as the plot. I want to write a good novel that happens to be a detective story, not just a detective story that happens to be a novel.'

This ambition has sometimes led to accusations that James is trying to raise the status of crime fiction above its station, a charge she denies. 'I don't think there is any need to be defensive about crime fiction,' she says. 'It has a long and honourable history, from Wilkie Collins and Arthur Conan Doyle to Dorothy L. Sayers and Raymond Chandler. It is a form that can accommodate serious moral and social themes. But it is also a demanding form. A new crime writer often thinks that because the genre is popular, it must be easy to write. They soon discover that structuring a complex plot, keeping the reader guessing, and maintaining suspense while also developing realistic characters is a formidable challenge. It is much harder than it looks.'

For James, the key to a successful crime novel lies in the tension between order and chaos. 'A crime novel is essentially about the disruption of a social order by a violent act, and the subsequent efforts to restore that order,' she explains. 'In that sense, it is a very conservative form. But it is also a very satisfying one. In a world that is increasingly chaotic and unpredictable, where we are constantly bombarded with news of violence and injustice that is never resolved, the crime novel offers a comforting narrative arc. A crime is committed, it is investigated, and it is solved. Order is restored. That is why it is so popular. It provides reassurance.' [line 41]

This sense of order is reflected in James's own life and work. Her study is meticulously organized, with reference books arranged alphabetically and drafts of her current novel filed neatly in colour-coded folders. Her prose style is similarly ordered — precise, clear, and 'uncluttered' by unnecessary ornament. [line 61] 'I like things to be in their place,' she admits. 'I think that is a characteristic of many crime writers. We are, by nature, organizers. We want to make sense of the world, even if it is only the world of our own creation.'

Despite her reputation for meticulous planning, James says she still enjoys the element of discovery in writing. 'I know the beginning and I know the end, and I have a very clear idea of the characters,' she says. 'But the journey between the two is always full of surprises. Sometimes a character will do or say something that I hadn't planned, and I have to follow them down a new path. That is the joy of writing. If I knew exactly what was going to happen on every page, I would get bored, and if I am bored, the reader will be bored too.'

Now in her eighties, James shows no signs of slowing down. She still writes every morning, sitting at her desk by seven o’clock. 'I prefer the morning,' she says. 'The mind is fresher then, and the house is quiet. By afternoon, the world starts to intrude — phone calls, letters, visitors. But in the early morning, I can live entirely in my own world. It is the only place I really want to be.'

sections.title: Part 4: Multiple Matching (Reading)
sections.instructions: You are going to read the introduction to a book about organising local events. For questions 20–34, choose from the sections (A–E). The sections may be chosen more than once.
sections.passageHtml:
Organising Local Events

Sarah Passingham, a professional fundraiser, offers advice on how to organise successful local events.

Section A

I have worked with a good many groups over the years, and I have seen events run beautifully and others turn into disasters. When things go wrong, it is desperately disappointing and disheartening for everyone involved. Perhaps by looking at some of my suggestions and learning from my mistakes, most of those disappointing times can be averted. However, this guide is not meant as the definitive work on organising events; rather it is meant as a framework on which to hang your own ideas and methods of doing things. One major difference between my own position and that of most local organisers is that I was being paid for my many hours of toil, whereas the vast majority of local volunteers work for no financial remuneration whatsoever.

Section B

Organising an event is hard work, and there have been times when I have felt so overwhelmed that I came close to falling apart on occasions. Sometimes what the volunteers were experiencing was far removed from the original concept we had planned, but nobody minded and, more often than not, nobody knew. It is easy to get caught up in the details and lose sight of the big picture, but I hope this book will act as a buffer between you and the chaos, and that it will allow you not only to appear calm and well organised, but genuinely to be those things. The feeling of being in control of a major project, rather than being swept along by it, is incredibly satisfying.

Section C

Committees can be tiresome, unwieldy groups, and I have sat through meetings where some members seemed to enjoy nothing more than social chit-chat and had no real desire to do any actual work. However, if you have a committee that operates efficiently and decisively, it can relieve you of much of the time-consuming but necessary work, and allow you to get to grips with the real nitty-gritty of the event. It is always worth having at least a cashier or treasurer as a second person to keep an eye on your balance or banking. You never want to be put in the position of having the finger of doubt or suspicion pointed at you, and having someone else check the figures is a simple safeguard for everyone.

Section D

My natural instinct, I must admit, is to want to do everything myself, but an unwillingness to delegate is a fatal flaw in an event organiser. You need to identify the strengths of your volunteers and match them to the tasks. Some people care passionately about the cause and will work tirelessly, while others may simply want to offer their obvious muscle and brawn for heavy lifting on the day. Both are valuable, and a good organiser knows how to make use of both. If you try to do it all, you will not only burn out, but you will also alienate the very people who have offered to help you.

Section E

Volunteers need to know that their contribution is valued and that they are not just being taken for granted. Always show that you need your volunteers, and thank them publicly and privately. In my experience, as long as they are prepared beforehand, people will have a go at almost anything, but they do need clear guidelines and a sense of direction. Some people actually prefer allowing someone else to hold the ultimate responsibility, finding it a relief to simply follow instructions. By establishing a clear chain of command, you can ensure that everyone knows what they are doing and who they should go to if a problem arises.

sections.passageHtml:
Secretaries

What's in a name? In the case of the secretary, or Personal Assistant (PA), it can be something rather surprising. The dictionary calls a secretary 'anyone who (0) handles correspondence, keeps records and does clerical work for others'. But while this particular job (1) ..... looks a bit (2) ....., the word’s original meaning is a hundred times more exotic and perhaps more (3) ...... The word itself has been with us since the 14th century and comes from the mediaeval Latin word secretarius meaning 'something hidden'. Secretaries started out as those members of staff with knowledge hidden from others, the silent ones mysteriously (4) ..... the secret machinery of organisations.

Some years ago 'something hidden' probably meant (5) ..... out of sight, tucked away with all the other secretaries and typists. A good secretary was an unremarkable one, efficiently (6) ..... orders, and then returning mouse-like to his or her station behind the typewriter, but, with the (7) ..... of new office technology, the job (8) ..... upgraded itself and the role has changed to one closer to the original meaning. The skills required are more demanding and more technical. Companies are (9) ..... that secretarial staff should already be (10) ..... trained in, and accustomed to working with, a (11) ..... of word processing packages. Professionals in the (12) ..... business point out that nowadays secretarial staff may even need some management skills to take on administration, personnel work and research.

Example (0): A. deals | B. handles | C. runs | D. controls (Answer: B)

Changing cities

What will the city of (0) the future look like? This question has been asked (13) ..... many times in recent history — and answered inconclusively (14) ..... equal number of times — that we (15) ..... be sure of (16) ..... thing only: no one can predict with (17) ..... degree of accuracy how cities will look 50 or 500 years from now.

The reason is simple — cities are (18) ..... a continual state of change. Over the (19) ..... fifty years they have changed so rapidly that the oldest residents will remember a time (20) ..... their city seemed to belong not just to another era (21) ..... to a different dimension.

(22) ..... is true both of planned and unplanned cities. Planned cities such as New York and Paris, (23) ..... are closely organised on a grid or diagram of streets and avenues, have effectively burst at the seams this century, while unplanned cities such as Tokyo and Los Angeles have grown just (24) ..... dramatically. Although their centres might remain much as they were many years (25) ....., their suburbs have spread (26) ..... the tentacles of an octopus.

Some economists argue that expansion is a sign of a healthy economy (27) ..... that it is expanding cities that attract international investment.

Example (0): THE
